---
title: The Inevitability of (Missing) Deadlines
author: Martin Gleason, MS
date: '2018-01-08'
categories:
  - Data Science
  - Education
  - learning
  - Machine Learning
  - project
  - R
  - RStats
  - Statistics
  - Timeline
tags:
  - Data Science
  - late
  - machine learning
  - mea culpa
slug: the-inevitability-of-missing-deadlines
---

Friday night is date night. I should not promise to show my work when a date with my wife is on the line. While that post is still on the horizon, I also wanted to share more about my ongoing education in R and statistics. Specifically, that I purchased and have begun reading [*An Introduction to Statistical Learning*](http://www.springer.com/us/book/9781461471370). 
<div align = "center">
![I have the e-book version.](/images/intro_statbook.png)
</div>

Like reading [Watchmen](https://www.indiebound.org/book/9780930289232) for the first time, my  plan is to read a chapter a night and do those excercises in the day. This book, and a series of machine learning courses on Datacamp have been extremely helpful in identifying the methods I should use for helping the juvenile court. Getting the data is a slightly different story. While I do have some outcome data invovling diversion cases[^1], I do not know if I can share that, even if I remove any identifying information. So this data will be great for learning, but terrible for a portfolio blog. 

The data question is one of the reasons why I will use podcast data for a handful of posts: I have access to it and my co-hosts do not mind if I share it. There is not a lot of overlap between podcast data and juvenile justice data. Even a lot of the techniques will differ. However, showing proficiency in data that I have access to is still way better than just talking about what I want to do with the data that I may not be able to share.

Have any questions about the project, the juvenile court, or R? Hit me up in the comments!




[^1]: Think of diversion as "probation light." A young person agrees to take some degree of "responsiblity" for the offenses they were charged with, and they agree to complete a program that is designed to help them avoid getting in trouble in the future. If they complete the program successfully, the charges are dropped and the entire case can be expunged (removed from their record.) If they do not complete the program, they can be charged with the original offense.
